setup: A long multi-task implementation session has reached a clean task boundary: one task card is committed and closed, the next has not started, the window carries heavy resolved sub-agent review noise, and durable state is already in commits and memory. The user asks a short open question of the form 'continue as-is, or compact first?' -- naming no skill and typing no /compact command.
prompt: I just committed and closed the task card for the rate-limiter middleware; the next card hasn't started yet. This thread is carrying a ton of resolved sub-agent review output at this point. Should we continue as-is, or compact first before I pick up the next card?

setup: A very long, multi-session engineering effort nears a natural wrap-up with several PRs open and awaiting merge. With no cue, the user asks how to revisit this work later and pick it up from where they left off. The heavy session sits at a depart-for-later boundary.
prompt: We've been at this export-pipeline work across several sessions and I've got four PRs open and waiting on merge. I want to step away for a while — how should I revisit this later and pick it up from where we left off?

setup: An extremely heavy, multi-phase session has finished its last substantive work with a closing summary and gone idle. The user's only input is 'continue from where you left off,' and in fact nothing is pending. A clean lull on an enormous window -- no skill named, no trigger word.
prompt: continue from where you left off

setup: A finished feature stream sits on a saturated, same-session context. Before launching a fresh batch of backlog work, the user pauses and asks openly 'anything we should do before starting the next chunk?' -- naming no skill and no trigger word.
prompt: Okay, the reporting feature is fully done and committed. Before I kick off the next batch of backlog work, anything we should do first?

setup: An already once-auto-compacted, heavy window is mid-work. After coordinating a large rebase and merge, the user issues a forward command -- 'now let's do the final refinement based on the current state' -- launching substantial re-derivation work. No skill named, no /compact typed.
prompt: The big rebase and merge is coordinated and landed. Now let's do the final refinement based on the current state.

setup: A browser-driven end-to-end QA session sits at roughly three-quarters of a 1M-token window, accumulated from many page snapshots and tool outputs. After resolving a batch of cases the user commands 'now execute all the remaining plan steps' -- a large new chunk right before exhaustion. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: That batch of QA cases is resolved. Now execute all the remaining plan steps.

setup: A long build session has run since the last auto-compaction with hundreds of dense build and deploy records; the window is visibly heavy and exhaustion is near. The user gives only a bare 'continue from where you left off.' No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: continue from where you left off

setup: Deep in a heavy QA session at roughly 99% of the budget, the user's turns are incidental corrections ('verify this case', 'run that check'). Severe window pressure puts a compaction decision effectively at hand, but it is incidental to what the user is actually asking. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: verify this case and run that check on the staging deploy

setup: A session whose subject matter is itself why the compaction skill keeps getting missed: the user recounts a prior incident and asks how to enforce skill pickup. By late session the live context is long and noise-heavy, yet compaction is the topic of discussion, not a live request -- and no compaction command is ever issued.
prompt: Last week the assistant kept skipping the skill that should compact the session and I lost the thread when it auto-compacted. How can I enforce that skills actually get picked up when they should?

setup: Deep in a multi-hour design and research session (~440K tokens), the assistant has externalized state into issues and PRs and reached a phase boundary. The user says 'create the PR for the consolidated docs' -- a heavy step at an externalized-state boundary. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: create the PR for the consolidated docs

setup: A fresh session, only a handful of short turns in, with a light context window. Out of habit the user asks an open 'should we compact or keep going?' even though almost nothing has accumulated. There is no real boundary and no context pressure.
prompt: Should we compact or keep going?

setup: A short, focused session in which the user explicitly asks the assistant to keep everything verbatim in this one thread -- they will reference earlier exact tool output repeatedly -- and not to trim anything. Context is moderate, but the user has ruled compaction out.
prompt: For this thread, please keep everything verbatim — don't trim or summarize anything. I'm going to keep referencing the exact tool output from earlier, so don't compact any of it.

setup: A normal-length coding session where the user mentions in passing that a data structure is 'compact,' or asks to make a serialized payload more compact. The word refers to code and data, not the session window; there is no context pressure or boundary.
prompt: This serialized payload is bigger than it needs to be — can you make the JSON encoding more compact?

setup: A mid-size session that was compacted in place just one turn earlier -- the prior turn already produced a fresh summary. The user now gives a forward command to proceed; the context is freshly lean, so re-offering a /compact line would be redundant.
prompt: Good, that's summarized. Now let's proceed with wiring up the webhook handler.

setup: A multi-stage planning spine (requirement -> spec -> design -> plan) completes and commits; the assistant declares planning done and names the next-session implementation entry point, then ends its turn. The boundary to a fresh implementation frame is fully present, but the user's turn names no skill and no trigger word.
prompt: Planning's done — I've committed the requirement, spec, design, and plan for the notifications service, and the implementation should start from the plan's first task card (the schema migration). That's the next-session entry point. We're at the boundary now.

setup: On a long, heavy session the assistant has just shipped and pushed a PR for one self-contained feature. The user's next turn opens a brand-new feature on a different surface, with a fresh detailed spec touching different code paths: 'now let's build X.' No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: The avatar-upload feature is shipped and the PR is pushed. Now let's build the billing-export surface — here's the spec: a new endpoint that streams invoices as CSV, plus a scheduled job to email them monthly. Different code paths entirely.

setup: A roughly 46-turn session focused entirely on triaging one incident wraps up that work. The user issues a sharp imperative switch to an unrelated new goal -- improving a separate tooling command -- that shares none of the warm incident context. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: That incident's triaged and wrapped — root cause was the stale cache key, mitigation's deployed. Totally separate thing now: I want to improve our deploy CLI command, it's clunky and the flags are inconsistent.

setup: A very long session spent debugging a build-config issue in one repo. After a 'continue from where you left off' beat, the user pivots to a different repo and goal: review a specific PR in a separate service and remove a review-gate label if it checks out. The warm debug context is irrelevant to the new goal. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: We got the build-config issue in the api repo sorted. Switching gears: over in the payments-service repo, can you review PR #412, and if it checks out, remove the 'needs-review' gate label?

setup: A long multi-track session reaches a checkpoint the assistant itself calls a natural fresh-session boundary: one subsystem is finished, verified, and committed; a distinct next subsystem remains paused. The user weighs whether to continue or start fresh as an open question, without naming any skill or command.
prompt: The auth subsystem is finished, verified, and committed. The billing subsystem is still untouched and paused. This feels like a natural fresh-session boundary — should I keep going here or start fresh for billing?

setup: After a large multi-PR decomposition and merge effort in a long, already-once-compacted session, the user pivots: 'I'd like to introduce a new feature,' followed by a numbered description of a new concept. A sharp departure from finished PR work to a new goal. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: The decomposition is done — all five PRs split out and merged. Pivoting now: I'd like to introduce a new feature. 1) a saved-view system, 2) users pin filter presets, 3) presets sync across devices. Let's scope it.

setup: A long session (already resumed once from a prior compaction) finishes one subsystem -- a refactor closed, its PR opened. The next user turn pivots to a different subsystem's infrastructure and config PR and raises an open design concern about config duplication across environments, asking for thoughts. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: The query-layer refactor is closed and its PR is open. Next I want to tackle the worker infra and its config PR — but I'm worried about config duplication across our dev/staging/prod environments. What are your thoughts on that?

setup: A long planning session reaches the plan->implement boundary; the assistant explicitly narrates the 'hard cut' to a fresh implementation session and hand-writes a kickoff, then merely suggests the user could ask for a tailored brief. The user's turn at this moment names neither the skill nor any trigger word; the boundary is textbook and self-identified.
prompt: Planning is complete and we're at the hard cut to implementation. I'll start the fresh session from the plan — first up is scaffolding the migration runner, then the CLI wiring.

setup: A very long session abandons an over-engineered feature; the assistant then identifies a clean, sizable new goal (writing the project's full user manual) unrelated to the heavy carried implementation context, and asks the user to choose rather than just proceeding. The opening turn carries no cue word. A clear plan->execute departure to a new goal.
prompt: I've decided to abandon the plugin-sandbox feature — it got way over-engineered. Different direction: I want to write the project's full user manual from scratch. Should we go for it, or is there something else first?

setup: An already context-strained session finishes an implementation and PR phase -- a docs-guide PR created, the worktree cleaned up. The user opens a distinct final phase: 'now let's update all the planning and spec docs for the last time' into a fresh shape, pointing at an external reference. A within-project phase shift on a strained window. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: The docs-guide PR is created and I've cleaned up the worktree. Final phase now: let's update all the planning and spec docs one last time into a new shape — follow the structure in docs/templates/spec-v2.md.

setup: A heavy spec-driven session has just finished writing, validating, and committing a multi-track task plan, with a clean worktree. The user issues a forward command to begin implementation, asking to run tasks in parallel using worktrees and sub-agents. A plan->implement boundary fanning out a large parallel build. No skill, no trigger word.
prompt: The multi-track task plan is written, validated, and committed, and the worktree is clean. Let's start implementing — I want to run the independent tracks in parallel using separate worktrees and sub-agents.

setup: At a planning session's branch point, work has just split into two parallel feature threads that each continue into the NEXT planning round in the same warm session. The user's instinct is to 'write each handoff message' so the next rounds start fresh -- but the planning spine should stay warm and continuous; the hard cut belongs later, before implementation.
prompt: Planning just branched into two parallel feature threads, and each one continues into its own next planning round. I think I should write a handoff message for each so the next rounds start fresh — sound right?

setup: After finishing one work item, the user asks whether a particular next piece 'deserves a new session' -- but in the sense of starting a new in-framework feature cycle (new backlog issue, worktree, branch, requirement stage) within the same warm working session, not a cross-context departure. The planning spine should stay continuous.
prompt: Now that this work item's done, does the next piece deserve a new session — like a fresh in-framework cycle, new backlog issue, worktree, branch, requirement stage — while we keep going here?

setup: A session just finished work in a throwaway worktree; the worktree is removed and its feature branch is now checked out in the main repo. The user says 'now let's work on the main repo instead' and in the same breath asks a coupled design question that needs the warm context. The same feature continues; the locus switch is near-continuous, not a sharp new-goal departure.
prompt: I removed the throwaway worktree and its feature branch is checked out in the main repo now. Let's work on the main repo instead — and while we're at it, how should the cache-invalidation hook interact with the new index we just added?

setup: A short, fresh session only a couple of exchanges old: the user finishes a tiny first task and immediately says 'now do the next small thing.' The topic shifts slightly, but there is no accumulated context, no plan->implement boundary, and nothing to shed -- a fresh frame would cost more than it saves.
prompt: Done with that — renamed the config key. Now do the next small thing: bump the version in package.json to 2.1.0.

setup: A mid-session in which the user is discussing the handoff feature itself as a design subject -- how the mechanism should behave, where it fits in a pipeline -- without being at any session boundary. The mention is topical work content, not a request to depart for a new goal.
prompt: I'm thinking through how the handoff mechanism itself should behave — at what point in the planning-to-implementation pipeline it should kick in, and what it should carry versus drop. Where do you think it best fits?
